overview_hero_desc: Parliamentary procedure exists to give every member an equal voice. Hatsell makes it effortless.
overview_familiar_p1: Your organization adopted parliamentary procedure years ago. In theory, everyone has an equal voice. In practice, it doesn't feel that way.
overview_familiar_p2: The chair fumbles through half-remembered scripts. A few confident members dominate the floor while others sit in silence, unsure when they're allowed to speak or how to formally disagree. Someone makes a motion, but nobody's quite sure if it needs a second, whether it can be amended right now, or what vote threshold applies. The secretary scribbles notes and hopes for the best.
overview_familiar_callout1: You leave the meeting suspecting that decisions were made more by confusion than by consensus.
overview_familiar_h2: "We don't really have rules at all..."
overview_familiar_p3: Your group meets regularly — a board, a club, a committee, a community council. There's an agenda, loosely followed. Whoever talks loudest or longest tends to win. Side conversations derail the discussion. When a decision is finally reached, half the room isn't sure what was actually decided.
overview_familiar_callout2: You leave the meeting frustrated that an hour was spent and nothing clear was accomplished.
overview_whatif_title: What if the rules just... ran themselves?
overview_whatif_p1: Parliamentary procedure exists to solve exactly these problems. It guarantees every member an equal right to speak, to propose ideas, to disagree, and to vote. It ensures that decisions are clear, recorded, and final.
overview_whatif_p2: The problem was never the rules. It was that the rules lived in a 700-page book, and nobody had time to memorize them.
overview_whatif_callout: Hatsell puts the rules in everyone's hands — automatically.
overview_how_intro: Hatsell is a real-time web app that guides your meeting through proper parliamentary procedure, step by step. The chair doesn't need to memorize scripts. Members don't need to know the rules. The app tells everyone what they can do, when they can do it, and what happens next.
overview_how_code_desc: The chair creates a meeting and gets a set of role codes — one for the chair, one for the vice chair, one for the secretary, and one for members. Share the member code and everyone joins from their own phone or laptop. No accounts, no sign-ups, no downloads.
overview_how_guide_desc: At every stage of the meeting, Hatsell shows the chair exactly what to say and do. Call the meeting to order. Conduct roll call. Ask for approval of minutes. Move through the agenda. The chair follows the prompts — the procedure takes care of itself.
overview_how_options_desc: When it's time to act, members see clear buttons: make a motion, second a motion, request to speak in favor or against, vote aye or nay. If an action isn't available right now — because someone else has the floor, or because the motion needs a second first — the button simply isn't there. No guessing, no interrupting, no awkward silence.
overview_how_debate_desc: Want to speak? Join the queue. Hatsell tracks who has spoken, how many times, and for how long. The chair recognizes speakers in order. Both sides get heard. When debate is done, the vote is called.
overview_how_decisions_desc: Votes are counted instantly and displayed to everyone. The result is announced. The meeting log captures every motion, every vote, every outcome. When the meeting ends, there's a clear record of what happened and what was decided.
overview_fivemin_intro: Here's what a typical Hatsell meeting looks like:
overview_step1_desc: The chair creates a meeting, configures a few settings (organization name, quorum, debate time limits), and shares the member code. Everyone joins.
overview_step2_desc: The chair taps one button. The meeting begins.
overview_step3_desc: The secretary calls names. Members tap "Present" on their screens. Quorum is confirmed automatically.
overview_step4_desc: A member makes a motion: "I move that we allocate $500 for a community bulletin board." Another member seconds it. The chair opens debate. Two people speak — one in favor, one against. The chair calls the vote. Members tap Aye or Nay. The motion passes, 4 to 1.
overview_step5_desc: No further business. The chair adjourns the meeting. The log shows everything that happened.
overview_fivemin_callout: Total time: as long as your business takes. No longer.
overview_noneed_title: What you don't need to know
overview_noneed_p1: You don't need to own a copy of Robert's Rules. You don't need to know what "subsidiary motion" means. You don't need to memorize when a motion is debatable or what vote it requires.
overview_noneed_p2: Hatsell knows. It encodes the rules of parliamentary procedure so you can focus on what actually matters: the ideas your group is discussing and the decisions you're making together.
overview_noneed_p3: If you want to go deeper — to understand why the rules work the way they do — Hatsell won't stop you. But you'll never be blocked because you forgot a technicality.
overview_ready_desc: Choose the guide that matches your role. Or just open Hatsell and follow the prompts — that's what they're there for.
overview_card_chair_desc: Creating a meeting, running each stage, managing debate, handling interruptions, and adjourning.
overview_card_member_desc: Joining, making motions, speaking in debate, voting, and using the tools available to you.
chair_hero_desc: You're running the meeting. That might sound intimidating — but Hatsell does the heavy lifting. You just follow the prompts.
chair_create_step1: Open Hatsell and enter your name.
chair_create_step3: You'll land on the General Settings screen. Fill in what you know — everything has sensible defaults.
chair_org_name_desc: Shows in the top bar during the meeting (e.g., "Maplewood Neighborhood Association").
chair_org_membership_desc: Used to calculate quorum. Leave blank if you're not sure.
chair_org_quorum_desc: The minimum number of members needed to do business. The default (majority of membership) follows Robert's Rules. You can set a fixed number or fraction if your bylaws say something different.
chair_org_voting_desc: How votes are counted. The default ("votes cast") is correct for most organizations. Only change this if your bylaws specify otherwise.
chair_org_debate_desc: How long each person can speak (default: 10 minutes) and how many times (default: 2). You can also set a total debate time limit per motion.
chair_settings_agenda_desc: Optionally add agenda items. You can use them as guidance (a reference for yourself) or as adopted Orders of the Day (binding).
chair_settings_notice_desc: Check any items that members were notified about in advance. This affects what vote thresholds apply.
chair_settings_type_desc: Regular or special. Special meetings can only discuss business specified in the call.
chair_settings_callout: Don't overthink the settings. The defaults work for a standard meeting. You can always come back and adjust for next time.
chair_codes_step1: Tap Confirm. Hatsell generates a unique code for each role: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary, and Member.
chair_codes_step2: Share the appropriate code with each person. The Member code is the one most people need. You can tap Copy All Codes to paste them into a group chat or email.
chair_running_intro: Once the meeting starts, you'll see three things that will be your constant companions:
chair_companion_guidance_desc: Tells you what to say and what to do next. This is your script.
chair_companion_actions_desc: Prominent buttons for whatever decision the meeting needs from you right now.
chair_companion_topbar_desc: Shows the meeting stage, organization name, and drawer toggles for participants, speaking queue, and meeting log.
chair_order_desc: The guidance box shows your opening script. Tap Call to Order. That's it — the meeting has begun.
chair_roll_desc: The secretary (or you, if there's no secretary) calls each member's name by tapping their card. Members tap Present on their own screens. Once everyone has responded, you'll see a button to Complete Roll Call.
chair_roll_callout: If you set a quorum, Hatsell tells you whether quorum is met. If it's not, you can't proceed to business — the app will guide you.
chair_minutes_desc: If this isn't your first meeting, the guidance prompts you to ask for approval of the previous meeting's minutes. Members can submit corrections. You can accept corrections by general consent or put them to a vote.
chair_business_intro: This is where the real work happens.
chair_business_step1: A member makes a motion. They tap their "Make a Motion" button and type what they propose. You'll see it appear as a pending motion.
chair_business_step2: You recognize it. Tap the motion to bring it to the floor. (If it's clearly out of order, you can reject it with an explanation.)
chair_business_step3: Another member seconds. The motion needs a second before debate can begin. If nobody seconds, you declare "no second" and the motion dies.
chair_business_step4: You state the question. The guidance gives you the script: "It is moved and seconded that [motion text]. Is there any debate?"
chair_business_step5: Members debate. They join the speaking queue by tapping "Speak in Favor" or "Speak Against." You recognize them in order. The speaking timer tracks their time.
chair_business_step6: You call the vote. When debate is done (or a member moves to close debate), tap Call the Vote. Members vote Aye, Nay, or Abstain on their screens.
chair_business_step7: You announce the result. Hatsell counts the votes and tells you whether the motion passed or failed. Tap Announce Result and the guidance gives you the script.
chair_int_poo_desc: A member thinks the rules are being broken. You'll see it immediately and must rule on it.
chair_int_inquiry_desc: A member has a question about procedure. Answer it and move on.
chair_int_amend_desc: A member wants to modify the motion on the floor. Amendments are debated and voted on before returning to the main motion.
chair_int_priv_desc: A member moves to recess or to adjourn. These take priority over regular business.
chair_interruptions_callout: Don't worry about remembering which of these is allowed when. Hatsell only shows members the options that are currently in order. If a button appears, they can use it.
chair_adjourn_desc: When there's no further business, tap Adjourn (or a member can move to adjourn). The meeting ends. The log contains a complete record of everything that happened.
chair_drawers_intro: Tap the buttons in the top bar to open:
chair_drawer_members_desc: Who's connected, their roles, and whether quorum is met. You can also set quorum here if you didn't set it during setup.
chair_drawer_queue_desc: The speaking queue, showing who's waiting and their stance (for/against).
chair_drawer_log_desc: A running record of every action taken in the meeting.
chair_guidance_title: What the guidance box does for you
chair_guidance_desc: The guidance box updates at every stage and transition. It tells you what to say out loud (in italic script text), what button to press next, and what the members are waiting for.
chair_guidance_callout: If you ever feel lost, read the guidance box. It always knows where you are.
chair_unexpected_title: When a member does something unexpected
chair_unexpected_p1: Parliamentary procedure has a response for everything. If a member raises a point of order, the guidance will tell you how to rule. If someone moves to table the motion, the app walks you through it. If an appeal is made against your ruling, Hatsell handles the vote.
chair_unexpected_p2: You don't need to anticipate these situations. Just respond when they come up — the app will guide you through.
chair_ref_order_desc: Tap the button, read the script
chair_ref_roll_desc: Wait for members to respond, then complete
chair_ref_business_desc: Recognize motions, manage debate, call votes, announce results
chair_ref_adjourn_desc: Tap Adjourn or recognize a motion to adjourn
chair_tip1: You don't need to talk fast. Parliamentary procedure is designed to be deliberate. Take your time.
chair_tip2: Read the scripts out loud. They're there for a reason — they set expectations for the members.
chair_tip3: Use the speaking timer. It keeps debate fair without you having to be the bad guy.
chair_tip4: Don't skip roll call for formal meetings. Quorum matters — it protects decisions from being challenged later.
chair_tip5: Check the log if you lose track. It records everything.
chair_tip6: Your first meeting will feel slow. That's normal. By the third meeting, the flow will feel natural.
member_hero_desc: You don't need to know parliamentary procedure — the app shows you exactly what you can do at every moment.
member_join_step1: Open Hatsell and enter your name.
member_join_step2: Enter the meeting code you were given and tap Join Meeting.
member_join_step3: You're in. You'll see the meeting screen with the current stage at the top.
member_join_callout: That's it. No account, no sign-up. The code determines your role — if you were given the Member code, you join as a member. If you were given the Secretary or Vice Chair code, you join with that role automatically.
member_flow_intro: The chair runs the meeting through a series of stages. You don't need to memorize them — just watch your screen for what's available to you.
member_roll_desc: The secretary (or chair) will call your name. When you see the prompt, tap Present. This confirms your attendance. The meeting can't proceed until enough members are present (that's called quorum).
member_minutes_desc: If there are minutes from a previous meeting, the chair will ask for approval. You have two options:
member_minutes_opt1: If the minutes look correct, do nothing — they'll be approved by general consent.
member_minutes_opt2: If something needs correcting, tap Submit Correction and describe what should change.
member_business_title: Business — this is where you matter most
member_business_intro: This is the heart of the meeting: proposals, debate, and decisions.
member_motion_p1: If you have a proposal — something you want the group to decide on — tap Make a Motion. Type what you're proposing in plain language (e.g., "Allocate $500 for a community bulletin board"). Submit it.
member_motion_p2: The chair will recognize your motion and bring it to the floor. Then it needs a second from another member before debate can begin.
member_second_desc: When someone else makes a motion, you'll see a Second button. Tapping it means "I think this is worth discussing" — it doesn't mean you agree. A motion needs at least one second to move forward.
member_speaking_intro: Once a motion is on the floor and open for debate, you can request to speak. You'll choose a stance:
member_speaking_desc: You're added to the speaking queue. The chair recognizes speakers in order. When it's your turn, a timer starts — speak your piece within the time limit. When you're done, tap Yield the Floor (or the timer will run out).
member_voting_intro: When debate is over, the chair calls the vote. Three buttons appear:
member_voting_desc: Tap once. Your vote is recorded. When everyone has voted (or the chair closes the vote), the result is announced.
member_other_intro: During business, you may have more options depending on what's happening. You don't need to memorize any of this:
member_other_callout: If an action is available, you'll see a button for it. If it's not available right now, the button won't be there.
member_amend_desc: You want to change the wording of the motion on the floor. Tap Amend, type your proposed change. It gets debated and voted on separately before the group returns to the original motion.
member_poo_desc: You think the rules are being broken (someone is speaking out of turn, the motion isn't in order, etc.). Tap Point of Order and describe your concern. The chair must stop and rule on it immediately.
member_inquiry_desc: You have a question about procedure ("Can we amend this motion?" or "How many votes does this need?"). The chair will answer.
member_info_desc: You need a factual clarification before you can vote ("What's our current budget balance?").
member_appeal_desc: You disagree with the chair's ruling on a point of order. An appeal puts the question to the whole group — majority rules.
member_adjourn_desc: You think the meeting should end. This gets voted on.
member_screen_stage_desc: The large heading at the top tells you where the meeting is (Roll Call, New Business, Voting, etc.).
member_screen_motion_desc: When a motion is on the floor, you'll see its text, who moved it, who seconded it, and its current status (debating, voting, etc.).
member_screen_actions_desc: These change based on what's happening. They're the only things you need to pay attention to. If there's something for you to do, there's a button for it.
member_screen_topbar_desc: Has buttons to open the participant list, speaking queue, and meeting log. Use these if you want to see who's in the meeting, where you are in the queue, or what's happened so far.
member_tip1: You can't break anything. The app only lets you do things that are in order. You can't accidentally speak out of turn or make an invalid motion.
member_tip2: You don't need to raise your hand. Tap the button. The queue handles it.
member_tip3: Seconding isn't agreeing. It just means "let's discuss this." You can second a motion and then vote against it.
member_tip4: If you're not sure, wait and watch. The first few minutes will make the rhythm clear. Most of the meeting is: someone proposes, people discuss, everyone votes.
member_tip5: Check the log if you missed something. It's in the top bar.
member_tip6: Your vote counts equally. That's the whole point.
